clean_story: Smooth gliding zippers.  Coils replace teeth. "Coil zippers" get their name from the spiral-shaped nylon or polyester coil that locks together—like two tiny springs twisting into one. Most zippers use metal or plastic teeth that click. Coil zippers slide silently, bending like a slinky to grip. No jagged edges, no snags. Here’s how they work:Two coils face each other on tape strips. Pull the slider—its Y-shaped channel forces the coils to interlock, one spiral nesting into the next. Like braiding hair with a single motion. They’re lighter than metal zippers, won’t rust, and flex around curves—perfect for jackets, luggage, or wetsuits. A 1950s innovation by Yoshida Kogyo (now YKK), now used in 90% of global apparel. Three related subjects are textile fasteners, polymer engineering, and slide mechanisms.
